companyCaseStudy.title: How Netflix Built Open Connect: Serving Tens of Tbps from the Edge and Cutting Origin Traffic by ~90%
companyCaseStudy.summary: Netflix serves 200+ million subscribers and peaks at tens of terabits per second of video delivery. To avoid paying the cloud and Internet transit tax for every frame, Netflix built Open Connect — a dedicated edge CDN that pushed video into ISPs, cut origin load by an order of magnitude, and improved startup and consistency for viewers.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Netflix streams billions of hours of video every year. During prime-time windows the service drives traffic measured in tens of terabits per second — and before Open Connect, almost all of that traffic hit Netflix's origins or third‑party CDN transit links. The bill (and the latency) was huge.

## Problem

- Scale: 200M+ subscribers and global primetime peaks in the tens of Tbps.
- Cost: Sending that much video across transit and cloud egress was enormously expensive.
- Performance: Long paths to origins increased startup time and rebuffering risk, harming QoE.

## Challenge

Netflix needed an architecture that could:
- Push huge amounts of traffic inside ISP networks (close to viewers).
- Manage and update terabytes of cached video efficiently.
- Keep overall system complexity and operational burden manageable, while supporting ABR (HLS/DASH), manifests, and regional catalogue differences.

## Solution: Open Connect (short story)

Netflix built Open Connect: a purpose-built CDN that places caching appliances inside ISP networks or at peering points. Key decisions:

- Appliance-first: Shippable cache appliances (OCAs) sized for hundreds of TB and multi‑10Gbps egress per appliance.
- ISP partnership & peering: Deploy appliances at ISP or IX locations so most viewers fetch segments from a nearby cache.
- Deterministic placement + global control plane: A central control plane decides which assets go to which POPs; caches fetch from origin or upstream caches only as needed.
- Chunked video storage + predictable eviction: Store video as segments; use popularity-driven placement and TTLs to maximize cache utility.
- Metrics & automation: Extensive telemetry on cache hit rates, egress, and QoE drives placement and capacity planning.

### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  Viewer[Viewer App] -->|HTTPS GET segment| ISP_POP[ISP/IX PoP - Open Connect Appliance]
  ISP_POP -->|Cache miss -> fetch| Regional_POP[Regional Open Connect Cache]
  Regional_POP -->|miss -> fetch| Origin[Netflix Origin Storage]
  ISP_POP --> Telemetry[Telemetry & Metrics]
  Telemetry --> ControlPlane[Open Connect Control Plane]
  ControlPlane --> ISP_POP
  ControlPlane --> Regional_POP
```

## Before vs After (high level)

| Metric | Before (Origin/Transit-heavy) | After (Open Connect) |
|--------|-------------------------------:|---------------------:|
| Origin egress | ~100% of viewer bytes | ~10% (order-of-magnitude drop) |
| Peak egress | Tens of Tbps to transit | Tens of Tbps from edge; far less upstream transit |
| Local cache hit ratio (per POP) | N/A | 70%–90% typical (varies by market & time) |
| Startup & rebuffering | Higher latency & more stalls | Lower latency, fewer stalls for most viewers |
| Transit cost | Very high | Tens of millions USD saved annually (order-of-magnitude) |

> Note: Netflix reports that the vast majority of its streaming traffic is served from Open Connect appliances in ISP networks — significantly reducing origin/transit egress.

## Why this worked (engineer's lens)

- Locality is everything for video. By moving large volumes of video to the ISP edge, round-trip and cross‑country hops vanish.
- Popularity is skewed. A relatively small fraction of titles drive most traffic; targeted placement and preloading make caches effective.
- Simplicity at scale. Using standardized appliances and an automated control plane allowed Netflix to operate hundreds of POPs and thousands of appliances reliably.

## Operational & technical specifics

- Appliances: OCA hardware is sized for large disk footprint and multi‑10Gbps NICs; a POP commonly has multiple appliances for redundancy and capacity.
- Control Plane: Centralized control plane decides which segments land where. It monitors telemetry, initiates preloads for hot titles, and retires stale content.
- Cache policy: Segment-level caching with popularity-driven prefetching and eviction minimizes cache churn.
- Peering strategy: Netflix negotiates direct peering with ISPs and places appliances inside ISP networks where possible — this both improves QoE and reduces provider transit costs.

## Results (concrete impact)

- Massive origin offload: most viewer traffic is served from Open Connect caches rather than origins or third-party transit.
- Cost reduction: by avoiding transit/egress for the majority of bytes Netflix significantly lowered bandwidth costs (public reporting and interviews indicate savings in the tens of millions USD annually; the exact figure varies by year and market).
- Improved QoE: viewers enjoy lower startup times and fewer rebuffer events because playback comes from a nearby appliance instead of a distant origin.
- Operational scale: hundreds of ISP PoPs and thousands of appliances deployed worldwide, serving tens of Tbps at peak.

## Tradeoffs and lessons

- CapEx vs OpEx: Open Connect invests in hardware and deployments (CapEx) to reduce long-term transit OpEx.
- Partnership overhead: requires negotiating placements and peering with ISPs, which takes time and relationship-building.
- Cache consistency: edge caching complicates instant global updates (but video is mostly read-heavy and versioned, which simplifies correctness).

## Visual summary flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[User requests video segment] --> B{Is segment on local OCA?}
  B -- Yes --> C[Serve from local OCA]
  B -- No --> D[Fetch from regional POP or origin]
  D --> C
  C --> E[Player continues playback]
  D --> F[Telemetry updates cache miss]
  F --> G[Control Plane may push/preload content to local OCA]
```

## Bottom line

Open Connect isn't just a CDN — it's an operational strategy: put large caches where your users are, automate placement based on popularity, and bake telemetry into every step. For massive video workloads, moving traffic to the edge is both a QoE and a cost win.
keyTakeaways: Push heavy read traffic to the edge: well-placed caches can reduce origin egress by an order of magnitude and serve tens of Tbps during peak.
Design for popularity skew: segment-level caching + prefetching for hot titles yields 70–90% local hit rates in many POPs.
Invest in telemetry and automation: central control + per‑POP telemetry enables preloading, capacity planning, and predictable QoE improvements.

concept.title: Don't Make the DB Do All the Work: Practical Caching Strategies (cache-aside, write-through, write-back)
concept.summary: Caching can cut latency, reduce load and save cost — but implemented badly it causes stale data, subtle bugs, and outages. This guide explains the three core caching strategies, when to use each, and practical pitfalls to avoid.
concept.explanation:
## Why this matters

Databases and upstream services are expensive and finite. When every request must consult the origin, latency spikes and costs balloon. Caching lets you serve repeatable data much faster and at lower cost — but there are tradeoffs between freshness, complexity, and durability.

**Analogy:** think of a restaurant kitchen. The database is the oven that bakes each dish to order. A cache is the prepped pick-up shelf where common orders sit ready. Do you only put popular finished dishes on the shelf, write every dish to the shelf as you make it, or let the shelf eventually get filled and occasionally flush it back to the oven? Each approach affects speed, waste, and correctness.

---

## The three core strategies (short summary)

- **Cache-aside (lazy loading):** App checks cache, on miss reads origin, then populates cache. Simple and common.
- **Write-through:** App writes both cache and origin synchronously on change. Cache always updated on writes.
- **Write-back (write-behind):** App writes to cache; the cache asynchronously persists to origin later. Fast writes, riskier durability.

---

## Sequence diagram: Cache-aside

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>App: request(key)
  App->>Cache: GET key
  Cache-->>App: MISS
  App->>DB: SELECT value
  DB-->>App: value
  App->>Cache: SET key value (with TTL)
  App-->>Client: value
```

## Flowchart: Write-through (reads from cache, writes update both)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Client Request] --> B{Read or Write?}
  B -->|Read| C[Check Cache]
  C -->|Hit| D[Return Cached]
  C -->|Miss| E[Read Origin -> Populate Cache -> Return]
  B -->|Write| F[Write to Origin]
  F --> G[Update Cache]
  G --> H[Return OK]
```

---

## Before / After: Quick impact example

| Metric | Before (no cache) | After (cache-aside) |
|--------|-------------------:|--------------------:|
| Average latency | 180 ms | 30 ms |
| DB QPS | 10,000 | 2,000 |
| Error rate during load spikes | 2% | 0.1% |
| Cost (monthly) | High | Lower |

These are illustrative numbers — your mileage will vary.

---

## Comparison table: pros, cons, when to use

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Cache-aside | Simple, flexible, lower cache churn | Application logic complexity, potential thundering herd on miss | Read-heavy systems, eventual consistency acceptable |
| Write-through | Simpler read consistency (cache reflects writes), less application code for updating cache | Higher write latency, writes tie up origin | When freshness on write is critical and write rate is moderate |
| Write-back | Fast writes, reduced origin write load | Risk of data loss on cache failure, complex failure handling, harder to reason about consistency | Very write-heavy systems that tolerate eventual durability and complex infrastructure for persistence guarantees |

---

## Practical examples (code)

### 1) Cache-aside (Node.js + Redis)

```js
// pseudo-code
async function getUser(userId) {
  const key = `user:${userId}`;
  const cached = await redis.get(key);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);

  const user = await db.query('select * from users where id=$1', [userId]);
  if (user) await redis.setEx(key, 60, JSON.stringify(user)); // TTL 60s
  return user;
}
```

Key points: populate cache only on miss; choose TTL; handle miss storms (see pitfalls).

### 2) Write-through (Node.js + Redis)

```js
async function updateUser(userId, payload) {
  // 1) update origin
  await db.query('update users set ... where id=$1', [userId]);
  // 2) update cache synchronously
  const key = `user:${userId}`;
  await redis.setEx(key, 300, JSON.stringify(payload));
}
```

Write latency includes DB plus cache update. Good for keeping cache fresh.

### 3) Write-back (conceptual)

- App writes to cache with a dirty flag and returns quickly.
- Background worker drains dirty items and persists to DB in batches.
- Need careful failure handling and eviction policy.

```text
// Pseudocode workflow
App -> cache.set(key, value, mark_dirty=true)
Background -> scan dirty keys -> persist batch to DB -> clear dirty flag
```

---

## Common pitfalls and how to mitigate them

- **Cache stampede (thundering herd):** Many clients miss simultaneously, hammer origin.
  - Mitigation: request coalescing (single-flight), randomized TTLs, locking on miss.

- **Stale data / inconsistency:** Cache contains old value after origin update.
  - Mitigation: use write-through or carefully invalidate cache after origin writes; use short TTLs if eventual consistency is acceptable.

- **Cache poisoning:** Incorrect or malicious values get cached.
  - Mitigation: validate responses before caching; avoid caching error responses.

- **Eviction surprises:** LRU evicts keys you assumed would stay cached.
  - Mitigation: monitor hit rates, size your cache, or pin hot keys.

- **Durability risks (write-back):** If cache node dies, you may lose recent writes.
  - Mitigation: use persistent write-ahead logs for write-back queues, replicate cache state, or prefer write-through where durability matters.

- **Operational complexity:** More layers to monitor and troubleshoot.
  - Mitigation: add metrics (hit/miss rates, TTL distribution), traces, and dashboards.

---

## When to pick which

- Use **cache-aside** for most read-heavy workloads where eventual consistency is fine and you want simple operations.
- Use **write-through** when reads must reflect recent writes and write rate is moderate; it simplifies correctness at the cost of write latency.
- Use **write-back** only when write latency must be minimized and you have infrastructure to guarantee durability and replay of pending writes.

---

## Operational checklist before rolling out caching

1. Set realistic TTLs and randomized TTL jitter.
2. Add metrics: hit rate, miss rate, origin QPS, stale hits.
3. Implement miss-coalescing for hot keys.
4. Define eviction and sizing policies and test under load.
5. Plan cache warming and invalidation strategies for deploys and failovers.

---

## Short troubleshooting playbook

- High origin load after cache rollout: check TTLs and cache hit rate.
- Inconsistent reads: verify invalidation on writes or consider write-through.
- Sudden drop in hit rate: investigate eviction, memory pressure, or wrong key prefixes.
keyTakeaways: Caching reduces latency and origin load but introduces tradeoffs between freshness, durability, and complexity.
Cache-aside is simplest for read-heavy workloads; write-through gives stronger freshness on writes; write-back optimizes write latency but requires robust durability guarantees.
Plan for stampedes, evictions, and monitoring; measure hit/miss rates and simulate failure scenarios before relying on cache for correctness.
